Title: Uber moves to acquire Delivery Hero in deal that could reshape global food delivery
URL: https://cryptobriefing.com/uber-delivery-hero-acquisition-negotiations/
Time Published: 2026-07-14T20:20:18Z
Full Content:
Searching... The ride-hailing giant is in advanced talks to buy the Berlin-based platform for what could be north of €10 billion, pausing European expansion plans to focus on the deal. Share Uber is looking to swallow one of Europe’s biggest food delivery companies whole. Delivery Hero confirmed on Monday that it is actively negotiating a potential acquisition by Uber Technologies, a deal that would represent one of the largest consolidation plays the food delivery industry has ever seen. The talks are described as advanced, with Uber reportedly aiming to finalize an agreement as early as this week. The transaction would involve an offer extended to all Delivery Hero shareholders, not just a partial buyout. Back in May 2026, Uber floated an indicative proposal that valued Delivery Hero at roughly €33 per share, which translated to a total market valuation of approximately €10 billion. But Delivery Hero’s stock has since been trading near €36 per share, suggesting the market expects Uber will have to pay more than its original offer indicated. Uber already holds an estimated 25% stake in Delivery Hero through a combination of direct holdings and options. Uber has paused its planned expansion of Uber Eats in five of the seven European markets it was targeting, specifically to concentrate resources on getting this acquisition across the finish line. Delivery Hero operates across Europe, Latin America, and parts of Asia and the Middle East/North Africa region. Acquiring the company would give Uber a footprint in markets where building from zero would take years. DoorDash has been aggressively expanding outside the United States, and Uber clearly doesn’t want to cede international territory. Meanwhile, Delivery Hero has been under pressure from activist investors and facing intensifying competition in its core markets. For Delivery Hero shareholders, paying above €36 per share means the total outlay could exceed the original €10 billion indicative valuation. The gap between the May indicative price of €33 and the current trading price near €36 suggests the market expects a sweetened offer. If negotiations stall or fall apart, that premium evaporates quickly. Title: Bank of America Announces Redemptions of $400,000,000 Floating Rate Senior Notes and $5,750,000,000 1.734% Fixed/Floating Rate Senior Notes, Due July 2027
URL: https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/bank-of-america-announces-redemptions-of-400-000-000-floating-rate-senior-notes-and-5-750-000-000-1-734-fixedfloating-rate-senior-notes-due-july-2027--302825538.html
Time Published: 2026-07-14T20:15:00Z
Full Content:
Searching for your content... In-Language News Contact Us 888-776-0942 from 8 AM - 10 PM ET Jul 14, 2026, 16:15 ET Share this article CHARLOTTE, N.C., July 14, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Bank of America Corporation announced today that it will redeem on July 22, 2026 all $400,000,000 principal amount outstanding of its Floating Rate Senior Notes, due July 2027 (CUSIP No. 06051GJV2) (the "Floating Rate Notes"), and all $5,750,000,000 principal amount outstanding of its 1.734% Fixed/Floating Rate Senior Notes, due July 2027 (CUSIP No. 06051GJS9) (the "Fixed/Floating Rate Notes" and, together with the Floating Rate Notes, the "Notes"). The redemption price for each series of the Notes will be equal to 100% of the principal amount of such series, plus accrued and unpaid interest to, but excluding, the redemption date of July 22, 2026. Interest on each series of the Notes will cease to accrue on the redemption date. Payment of the redemption price for the Notes will be made through the facilities of The Depository Trust Company. The Bank of New York Mellon Trust Company, N.A. is the trustee and paying agent for the Notes. Bank of America Bank of America is one of the world's leading financial institutions, serving individual consumers, small and middle-market businesses and large corporations with a full range of banking, investing, asset management and other financial and risk management products and services. The company provides unmatched convenience in the United States, serving nearly 70 million clients with approximately 3,500 retail financial centers, approximately 15,000 ATMs (automated teller machines) and award-winning digital banking with approximately 60 million verified digital users. Bank of America is a global leader in wealth management, corporate and investment banking and trading across a broad range of asset classes, serving corporations, governments, institutions and individuals around the world. As the #1 small business lender in the United States (FDIC), Bank of America offers industry-leading support to approximately 4 million small business households through a suite of innovative, easy-to-use online products and services. The company serves clients through operations across the United States, its territories and more than 35 countries and/or jurisdictions. Bank of America Corporation stock (NYSE: BAC) is listed on the New York Stock Exchange. Title: 10 Best AI Logo Generators to Create Professional Logos
URL: https://graphicdesignjunction.com/2026/07/best-ai-logo-generators/
Time Published: 2026-07-14T20:11:54Z
Full Content:
Creating a professional logo used to take days or even weeks. You had to hire a designer, explain your ideas, wait for drafts, request revisions, and finally receive the files. Today, AI logo generators have changed that process. In just a few minutes, you can create dozens of logo concepts for almost any type of business. Whether you’re launching a startup, opening a local coffee shop, starting an online store, or building a personal brand, an AI logo generator can help you create a professional-looking logo without spending hundreds of dollars. Of course, not every AI logo maker delivers the same results. Some tools generate modern and creative logos, while others produce designs that feel generic or outdated. Some give you full editing control, while others only let you download a finished design. Pricing, export options, and commercial licensing also vary from one platform to another. That’s why I decided to test some of the most popular AI logo generators available today. The best AI logo generators don’t just save time – they help transform ideas into professional brand identities in minutes. Instead of simply reading product pages or comparing feature lists, I created logos with every tool myself using the same prompts and business ideas. This made it much easier to see which AI logo generators produce the best results and which ones still need improvement. If you’re looking for the best AI logo generator in 2026, this guide will help you choose the right one for your next branding project. Download Now There are hundreds of articles online listing the “best AI logo generators“, but many of them simply repeat information from company websites without actually using the tools. I wanted this comparison to be different. Every AI logo generator included in this article was tested using the same workflow and similar prompts. Instead of judging them by marketing claims, I compared the actual logos they produced. The goal wasn’t to find the “perfect” logo. Instead, I wanted to answer a few simple questions: This hands-on approach gives a much more realistic comparison than simply listing features. To keep the comparison fair, I used nearly identical prompts and instructions across every platform. Each AI logo generator was asked to create logos for the same four business categories: These business types were chosen because they cover very different branding styles. A coffee shop usually needs a warm, friendly, and welcoming design. A real estate company often requires a clean, trustworthy, and professional logo. A fitness brand benefits from bold typography, energetic colors, and modern icons. A tech startup typically looks best with a simple, minimal, and futuristic visual identity. Using the same categories made it much easier to compare how each AI handled typography, colors, icons, layouts, and overall creativity. For every platform, I also explored the available customization options after the initial logo was generated. Some tools allow extensive editing, while others offer only basic color or font changes. Throughout this article, you’ll find 2 – 4 logo examples created with each AI logo generator. These examples use the same business ideas, making it easier to compare the quality of the generated logos side by side. The purpose of these examples is not to declare one tool as the absolute winner. Instead, they show how different AI systems interpret the same branding request. Not every logo generator is designed for the same audience. Some platforms focus on speed, allowing users to create a logo in less than five minutes. Others provide advanced editing features, making them more suitable for designers who want complete creative control. After testing these AI tools, I found that the best AI logo generators usually have a few things in common. First, they generate logos that don’t immediately look like they were created by AI. Good typography, balanced layouts, and thoughtful icon choices make a huge difference. Second, customization is just as important as the initial design. AI can provide a strong starting point, but most logos still need small adjustments before they truly represent a brand. Finally, export quality matters. A business logo should work on websites, business cards, social media profiles, packaging, merchandise, and large printed banners. Having access to vector formats like SVG or PDF ensures the logo stays sharp at any size. Let’s take a closer look at each platform, examine the logos they produced, and see where each one performs best. Best for: Professional designers, UI/UX teams, agencies, and startups that want complete control over their logo designs. Figma has become one of the most popular design platforms in the world, especially for UI and UX design. With its latest AI features, it is also becoming a useful tool for logo creation. Unlike many dedicated AI logo makers, Figma doesn’t simply generate a finished logo and stop there. Instead, it helps you brainstorm ideas, generate layouts, edit vector graphics, and continue refining your design inside the same workspace. During testing, Figma AI produced some of the cleanest logo concepts in this comparison. Typography looked balanced, spacing was consistent, and the generated ideas felt more modern than many traditional logo generators. What impressed me most was the flexibility after generation. Instead of accepting whatever AI creates, you can easily edit fonts, icons, colors, alignment, spacing, and shapes without leaving the application. If you already use Figma for website or app design, keeping your logo project inside the same software makes the workflow much faster. Figma offers a free plan with generous features. Advanced AI capabilities and professional collaboration tools are available through paid plans. Overall Rating: 4.9 / 5 After testing all four logo prompts, Figma AI consistently produced some of the strongest results in this comparison. While it isn’t a one-click logo generator, it gives designers much more freedom to refine and improve the final design. If you want a professional logo that doesn’t look generic, Figma AI is one of the best choices available. My Recommendation: If you’re serious about branding and don’t mind spending a little extra time editing your design, Figma AI deserves a place at the top of your shortlist. Best for: Beginners, marketers, content creators, and small businesses looking for fast AI-generated logos. Microsoft Designer has grown into much more than a social media design tool. Thanks to Microsoft’s investment in AI, it can quickly generate branding ideas, marketing graphics, and simple logo concepts from a short description. Unlike traditional logo makers that ask dozens of questions before generating a design, Microsoft Designer feels much more conversational. Simply describe your business, choose a style, and the AI begins creating visual concepts within seconds. During my testing, Microsoft Designer produced clean, modern logos with balanced layouts. Typography looked especially good for technology startups and fitness brands. Coffee shop logos also looked attractive, although a few icon choices felt a little generic. One thing I liked was the speed. It generated multiple ideas almost instantly, making it easy to experiment with different styles. Microsoft Designer is not a dedicated logo platform, so advanced editing options are more limited than Figma or Adobe Express. If you need complete control over every design element, you’ll likely export the logo and finish it elsewhere. Microsoft Designer includes many AI features for free, while additional AI capabilities are available through Microsoft subscriptions. Overall Rating: 4.5 / 5 Microsoft Designer is one of the easiest AI logo generators I tested. It creates clean logo concepts in seconds and requires almost no design experience. While it doesn’t replace professional branding software, it is an excellent starting point for entrepreneurs, startups, and creators. My Recommendation: Choose Microsoft Designer if you want a fast, beginner-friendly AI logo maker that also helps create social media graphics and other marketing assets. Best for: Designers, startups, agencies, and businesses that want creative AI-generated logos with access to a massive design library. Freepik has become one of the biggest platforms for designers, offering millions of vectors, icons, templates, photos, and AI-powered creative tools. Its AI Logo Maker is designed to help users generate professional logo ideas quickly while still providing enough flexibility to customize the final design. During my testing, Freepik AI Logo Maker produced some of the most visually appealing logos in this comparison. The designs felt modern, balanced, and suitable for real businesses rather than looking like generic clipart. I noticed that typography was handled particularly well. The AI paired fonts with icons in a way that looked natural, especially for coffee shops and technology startups. The fitness logos also had strong, energetic layouts without becoming overly complicated. One advantage of Freepik is its huge collection of design resources. If the generated logo needs additional editing, you can easily enhance it using Freepik’s icons, vectors, mockups, and other branding assets without leaving the platform. Although the AI does an excellent job creating first drafts, some logos still benefit from small manual adjustments to spacing, colors, or typography. Fortunately, those changes are easy to make. Freepik offers free AI generations with limits, while premium plans unlock additional logo generations, downloads, editing tools, and access to millions of creative assets. Overall Rating: 4.8 / 5 Freepik AI Logo Maker was one of my favorite tools during testing. It consistently generated attractive logo concepts with modern typography, strong icons, and balanced layouts. Combined with Freepik’s huge collection of design resources, it becomes much more than a simple logo generator. My Recommendation: If you want high-quality AI logo concepts and access to a complete creative ecosystem, Freepik AI Logo Maker is an excellent choice for designers, startups, and growing businesses. Best for: Beginners, entrepreneurs, freelancers, and small businesses that want an easy way to create professional logos. Canva has become one of the world’s most popular design platforms, and its Magic Design AI tools make creating logos easier than ever. Instead of starting with a blank canvas, you simply describe your business, choose a style, and let the AI generate several logo ideas within seconds. During my testing, Canva consistently created clean and attractive logo concepts. It performed especially well with coffee shop and lifestyle brands, producing modern typography and balanced layouts that looked ready for websites and social media. One of Canva’s biggest strengths is what happens after the logo is generated. Every element can be edited using Canva’s drag-and-drop editor. Fonts, colors, icons, backgrounds, spacing, and layouts can all be changed without any design experience. Because Canva includes thousands of templates, stock graphics, and branding tools, it’s easy to turn a simple logo into a complete visual identity. You can quickly create business cards, flyers, social media posts, presentations, and marketing materials using the same brand colors and typography. The only downside is that some premium fonts, icons, and export options require Canva Pro. Free users can still create attractive logos, but the best assets are reserved for paid plans. Canva offers a generous free plan. Canva Pro unlocks premium templates, Brand Kits, AI features, and additional export options. Overall Rating: 4.7 / 5 Canva Magic Design is one of the easiest AI logo generators for beginners. It creates attractive logo concepts quickly, and its editing tools make it simple to customize every part of the design. If you also need social media graphics, keynote presentations, or marketing materials, Canva becomes an excellent all-in-one branding platform. My Recommendation: Canva is a great choice for startups, freelancers, bloggers, and small businesses that want an affordable logo maker with powerful editing features. Best for: Creative professionals, marketers, and businesses that want polished logo concepts with Adobe’s AI tools. Adobe Express combines Adobe’s design experience with AI-powered tools to help users create logos, social media graphics, flyers, presentations, and complete branding materials. Powered by Adobe Firefly technologies, it aims to produce clean, modern designs while keeping the editing process simple. In my testing, Adobe Express consistently delivered professional-looking logo concepts. Typography felt balanced, icons matched the business type, and the overall layouts looked polished. The technology startup and real estate logos stood out the most because they had a premium, corporate feel. Unlike many one-click logo makers, Adobe Express lets you continue refining your design after the AI finishes. You can change fonts, colors, icons, backgrounds, spacing, and layouts without starting over. If you already use Photoshop, Illustrator, or other Adobe Creative Cloud apps, Adobe Express fits naturally into your workflow. It’s easy to move branding assets between applications and build a complete visual identity. Although beginners can use Adobe Express, the large number of design tools may feel overwhelming at first. After a short learning period, however, it becomes one of the most flexible AI branding platforms available. Adobe Express includes a free plan with basic features. Premium plans unlock advanced AI tools, Brand Kits, premium assets, and additional export options. Overall Rating: 4.5 / 5 Adobe Express AI was one of the strongest performers in this comparison. It consistently produced professional logos that required very little editing before they looked ready for real businesses. Combined with Adobe’s branding tools and Creative Cloud ecosystem, it offers excellent value for anyone building a complete brand identity. My Recommendation: If you already use Adobe products or want a polished logo with room for customization, Adobe Express AI is one of the best AI logo generators available today. Best for: Local businesses, entrepreneurs, and companies that need a logo plus matching printed marketing materials. VistaPrint is well known for business cards, flyers, packaging, signs, and other printed products. Its AI Logo Maker extends that experience by helping users create a logo that can immediately be used across both digital and print branding. During my testing, VistaPrint generated clean and professional logo concepts. The designs focused on readability and practical business use rather than flashy effects. The real estate and coffee shop logos looked especially polished, with balanced typography and simple icons. One thing I appreciated was how easy it is to move from logo creation to ordering branded materials. After generating a logo, you can quickly apply it to business cards, letterheads, apparel, stickers, and promotional products without recreating your branding. The customization tools are straightforward, allowing you to adjust fonts, colors, icons, and layouts. They aren’t as advanced as Figma or Adobe Express, but they’re more than enough for most small businesses. If your goal is to launch a business quickly with matching branding assets, VistaPrint offers a convenient all-in-one workflow. Creating logo concepts is simple, while premium logo downloads and printed branding products are available through paid packages. Overall Rating: 4.2 / 5 VistaPrint AI Logo Maker is built for business owners who want a simple branding solution. It may not have the deepest editing tools, but it creates professional logos quickly and makes it incredibly easy to turn those designs into real-world marketing materials. My Recommendation: Choose VistaPrint if you’re launching a business and need both a logo and matching printed products from one platform. Best for: Startups, website owners, freelancers, and entrepreneurs who want a logo and website branding in one place. Hostinger is best known for web hosting and website building, but it also offers an AI Logo Maker designed to help new businesses create their brand identity quickly. The tool focuses on simplicity, making it possible to generate a logo in just a few minutes without any design experience. During my testing, Hostinger produced modern and clean logo concepts. The interface is very beginner-friendly. After entering your business name and a short description, the AI creates several logo concepts that can be customized with different fonts, colors, layouts, and symbols. One of Hostinger’s biggest advantages is its ecosystem. If you’re building a website with Hostinger Website Builder, your logo can easily become part of your complete online brand without moving files between different platforms. Although professional designers may still prefer advanced software like Figma or Adobe Express, Hostinger AI Logo Maker offers an excellent balance between speed, simplicity, and quality. Hostinger AI Logo Maker is available as part of its website ecosystem, with premium options depending on the services you choose. Overall Rating: 4 / 5 Hostinger AI Logo Maker is an excellent choice for anyone starting a new website or online business. It combines quick AI-generated logo concepts with easy editing tools, making it possible to create a complete online brand without hiring a designer. My Recommendation: If you’re already planning to build a website with Hostinger, its AI Logo Maker is a convenient and practical way to create a matching brand identity. After testing all ten AI logo generators using the same prompts and business categories, I found that every platform has its own strengths. Some produced beautiful typography, while others focused on speed, customization, or beginner-friendly workflows. Instead of naming one tool as the winner for everyone, here are my recommendations based on different needs. AI logo generators have improved dramatically over the past few years. They are no longer just tools for creating quick placeholders. Many can now generate professional-looking logo concepts that are suitable for startups, freelancers, online stores, and even growing businesses. That said, every AI logo generator has its own strengths. Some focus on creativity, while others prioritize speed, customization, or branding tools. The right choice depends on your goals, budget, and how much control you want over the final design. My biggest recommendation is to treat AI as a creative partner rather than a replacement for good design. Use AI to generate ideas, explore different directions, and save time, then refine the final logo until it truly represents your brand. If you’re just getting started, Canva Magic Design, Hostinger AI Logo Maker, or Namecheap Logo Maker are excellent choices. If you’re working on professional branding projects, Figma AI, Adobe Express AI, and Freepik AI Logo Maker offer much greater creative flexibility. No matter which platform you choose, spend a little extra time customizing your final design. Small adjustments to fonts, colors, spacing, and icons can make a huge difference and help your logo stand out from thousands of other AI-generated designs. Based on my testing, Figma AI, Adobe Express AI, and Freepik AI Logo Maker produced the strongest overall results. Yes. Modern AI tools can generate professional-looking logos, especially for startups and small businesses. That depends on the platform. Always review the licensing terms before using a logo commercially. Trademark eligibility varies by country. Adding your own edits can help create a more distinctive design. Canva Magic Design and Microsoft Designer are both excellent for beginners. Namecheap Logo Maker and Shopify Hatchful both provide free logo creation options. Several tools, including Figma AI, Adobe Express AI, Freepik AI Logo Maker, and Canva Pro, support vector exports. If you need a unique brand identity with extensive research and strategy, hiring a designer is still the best choice. AI works well for generating ideas and creating an affordable starting point. Yes. Most platforms allow some level of editing, and you can also refine the logo using professional design software. I recommend creating at least 10–20 variations before choosing your favorite. Comparing multiple ideas often leads to a much stronger final logo. July 13, 2026• Logos, Inspiration July 10, 2026• AI Tools July 9, 2026• AI Tools July 8, 2026• Fonts, Typography Comments are closed. Graphic Design Junction is a valuable resource for graphic designers of all levels. Whether you are a beginner or a seasoned professional, you are sure to find something useful on the website.

Title: Lucid Motors denies report it's considering bankruptcy | TechCrunch
URL: https://techcrunch.com/2026/07/14/lucid-motors-denies-report-its-considering-bankruptcy/
Time Published: 2026-07-14T18:48:54Z
Full Content:
Latest AI Amazon Apps Biotech & Health Climate Cloud Computing Commerce Crypto Enterprise EVs Fintech Fundraising Gadgets Gaming Google Government & Policy Hardware Instagram Layoffs Media & Entertainment Meta Microsoft Privacy Robotics Security Social Space Startups TikTok Transportation Venture Staff Events Startup Battlefield StrictlyVC Newsletters Podcasts Videos Partner Content TechCrunch Brand Studio Crunchboard Contact Us Lucid Motors is denying a report that it’s weighing filing for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection. Nick Twork, the company’s chief communications officer, told TechCrunch in a statement that the “rumors are completely false.” “The company has sufficient liquidity to carry its operations well into next year, as recently published in its last quarterly filings, and it has not formed any special Board committee to explore the scenarios reported today,” he said. “Our focus is on improving execution, strengthening operations, and positioning Lucid to realize the full potential of its technology, products, and innovation.” Lucid’s denial comes after its stock price sunk more than 50% on Tuesday, its biggest intra-day drop ever, according to Bloomberg News. The stock has recovered from the free fall, and was trading at $4.72 a share as of 2:46 p.m. ET, about 14% lower than its opening price. The company recently named a new CEO and has laid off more than 2,000 employees this year as part of a sweeping restructuring ahead of the expected launch of its smaller, more affordable electric SUV later this year. Earlier Tuesday, an electric vehicle blog published a report citing two unnamed sources who said the company was considering either filing for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection or going private on the recommendation of consulting firm AlixPartners. Twork said AlixPartners is assisting Lucid on strengthening its operations and “nothing else and has not recommended bankruptcy to management or the Board.” AlixPartners has been a go-to consulting firm for struggling electric vehicle companies in recent years. Lordstown Motors tapped the firm in 2021 after its CEO and CFO resigned, in an attempt to restructure its nascent business. The startup ultimately partnered with Taiwanese electronics giant Foxconn, though that relationship soured and Lordstown Motors went out of business. Faraday Future also brought in AlixPartners to try and implement recommendations made by its board of directors in the wake of an internal probe in 2022. Lucid Motors recently revealed that it delivered 3,953 vehicles in the second quarter of this year, only slightly more than it shipped in the same period last year. It has historically struggled to find buyers for its luxury EVs, despite their impressive technological specs. Along with the latest round of layoffs announced earlier this month, Lucid also said it would eliminate a second production shift at its Arizona factory as it aligns “production plans with anticipated demand.” Lucid Motors is also trying to get a luxury robotaxi service off the ground by the end of this year with partners Uber and Nuro. Uber has committed to buying at least 35,000 Nuro-equipped Lucid Motors vehicles for the effort over the next few years, with 10,000 of those being Gravity SUVs, and 25,000 based on the upcoming midsize EV platform. Title: IBM’s Disappointing Earnings Are Shaking Up Wall Street. What Should Software Investors Do Now?
URL: https://www.investopedia.com/ibms-disappointing-earnings-shaking-up-wall-street-what-should-software-investors-do-12019075
Time Published: 2026-07-14T17:49:36Z
Full Content:
Get personalized, AI-powered answers built on 27+ years of trusted expertise. IBM says the AI-driven memory shortage is dinging its sales. That’s bad for the stock—and it’s pulling software shares downward. “Big blue” earlier today in a preliminary earnings report said revenue increased 1% to $17.2 billion in the second quarter, well below the 5% target the company has set for full-year growth. Software revenue growth decelerated to 5% from 11% in the prior quarter, and infrastructure revenue declined 7% after increasing 15% in Q1. IBM (IBM) CEO Arvind Krishna said the disappointing software and infrastructure performance was “driven by a shortfall in our Z performance and the associated software stack”—Z is the company’s name for its mainframe systems—and the company’s failure to adapt to market conditions, but he noted the memory shortage played a part, too. “In the last few weeks of June, we saw clients shift their quarterly capex spend toward servers, storage, and memory purchases to secure supply-constrained infrastructure ahead of expected price increases,” Krishna wrote. The company had anticipated some supply chain challenges, but “we did not anticipate the magnitude of the capex reprioritization,” he added. Tech investors have been debating the likelihood that AI empowers non-software companies to build their own custom tools rather than shell out for existing software that isn’t purpose-built. The industry’s headwinds grew stronger on Tuesday when IBM suggested the AI-fueled memory shortage is causing a greater share of IT budgets to be spent on hardware. IBM’s update adds to the software industry’s AI headwinds. Shares slumped earlier this year as investors debated whether increasingly capable AI coding agents would upend the high-margin software business. Those fears were amplified last week by a report that Starbucks is using AI to develop in-house tools to replace inventory and maintenance management software supplied by Microsoft and IBM. IBM shares plummeted 25% on Tuesday, leading a sell-off in software and tech services stocks. ServiceNow (NOW) and Adobe (ADBE) slid 6% and 4%, respectively, while Microsoft (MSFT) stock shed 1.5%. Meanwhile, shares of server, storage, and memory device makers rallied after yesterday’s slump. Shares of server makers Dell (DELL) and Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE) jumped 7% and 5%, respectively. Sandisk (SNDK) and Micron (MU) advanced about 5% each. Booming demand from AI data centers has caused a shortage of memory and data storage devices being felt across the tech sector. Chipmakers and analysts have warned since last year that soaring memory costs could pressure profits at tech hardware makers like Intel (INTC) and Dell. Apple (AAPL) last month raised prices on some products to offset higher costs. Memory suppliers like Micron and Sandisk have seen their sales and profits balloon in the past year, fueling big gains for their stocks. Tuesday’s IBM update may force a reset of investor expectations heading into a highly anticipated round of earnings reports seen as vital to inspiring more confidence in stocks. Analysts at Bank of America and Oppenheimer had recently predicted IBM would raise its full-year revenue forecast after reporting unsurprising second-quarter results. Goldman Sachs analysts forecast “modest upside” for the quarter “with software momentum likely intact.” “Checks show no surprises in business trajectory,” Oppenheimer analysts titled their IBM earnings preview published shortly before the company’s preliminary results surprised Wall Street. (IBM is slated to report official quarterly results after markets close on July 22.) The big question now: how much to read into today’s news. JPMorgan analysts in a note Tuesday suggested investors may be jumping the gun if they lower the bar for software earnings based on IBM’s results. The analysts warned against extrapolating IBM’s revenue headwinds, which stem from “customer spend on Mainframes and associated software,” not its entire software business, which includes cloud, automation, and data management tools. While JPMorgan analysts expect the capex prioritization highlighted by IBM will preclude big software earnings beats in the coming weeks, their recent check-ins with companies suggest “the headwinds do not appear to be broad-based.” Update—July 14, 2026: This article was updated after initial publication with stock performance data as of Tuesday’s market close.
--------------------------------------------------

Title: The Great Tech Rotation Just Triggered a Flawless Entry Point Into the World’s Ultimate Hardware Play
URL: https://247wallst.com/investing/2026/07/14/the-great-tech-rotation-just-triggered-a-flawless-entry-point-into-the-worlds-ultimate-hardware-play/
Time Published: 2026-07-14T17:39:36Z
Full Content:
Investing TSM controls ~70% of global foundry capacity and exclusively manufactures NVIDIA's Blackwell chips, with CEO C.C. Wei guiding 30%+ full-year revenue growth for 2026. TSMC's Arizona fab now qualifies for a 35% U.S. investment tax credit while Germany and Japan expansions actively engineer down the decade-long geopolitical risk premium. Act now: the analyst who called NVIDIA in 2010 just named his top 10 AI stocks — and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing didn't make the cut. Grab the names FREE today. Sending You to Google News in 3 © AlexSava / Getty Images I hit the buy button on Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (NYSE:TSM | TSM Price Prediction) again this week, and I will hit it again the next time macro traders panic. The recent pullback did not shake me. It handed me a discount on the one company that physically manufactures the future. Here is the thesis in plain English: every serious AI chip on earth runs through Taiwan Semiconductor’s fabs. NVIDIA’s Blackwell architecture, the accelerators from Apple, AMD, Broadcom, and the custom silicon quietly reshaping hyperscaler data centers all funnel into the same foundry. When a company commands roughly 70% of the global foundry market and serves as the exclusive producer for NVIDIA’s Blackwell architecture, it becomes infrastructure. That is what keeps pulling me back. The growth trajectory is doing the arguing for me. May 2026 consolidated net revenue landed at NT$416.98 billion, up 30.1% year over year, and cumulative revenue through the first five months of 2026 came in at NT$1.96 trillion, up 30.0%. CEO C.C. Wei has guided to more than 30% full-year revenue growth in 2026. That guidance is being backed by cash. Q1 2026 net income attributable to shareholders came in at $572.80 billion, up 43.82% year over year, on revenue of $1.134 trillion, up 21.45% YoY. Second, the moat is widening geographically. The TSMC Arizona fab is now eligible for a 35% U.S. investment tax credit, up from 25%, effective January 1, 2026. Add in government subsidies underpinning the Germany (ESMC) and Japan (JASM) plants, and the political risk premium that scared people for a decade is being actively engineered down. Third, the valuation. This is a foundry monopoly trading at a trailing P/E of 39 and a forward P/E of 31, with operating margins of 58.1% and return on equity of 36.2%. Wall Street currently sits at 17 Buy ratings, 2 Hold, and zero Sell ratings. When a business compounds like this and the analyst desk cannot find a bear, I stop trying to time the entry. Customer concentration is real. The top 10 customers account for 84% of accounts receivable. Layer that onto the geopolitical and earthquake exposure of Taiwan-based facilities, and you have a genuine tail risk. I do not dismiss it. What blunts it for me is the Arizona, Japan, and Germany expansion, the mutual dependency of those top customers (NVIDIA cannot switch fabs on a whim), and the fact that the U.S. government has quietly turned this company into a strategic partner rather than a foreign vendor. The stock is up 43.59% year to date and 1,943.54% over the past ten years, and I still think the retirement-account version of me will look back at $434 as a bargain. Every AI roadmap runs through this fab. I would rather own the road than guess which car wins. Contact [email protected] for any questions or corrections. Title: I asked Grok: “What do you think would happen if Bernie Sanders’ proposal for the government to own 50% of AI companies were to happen? What kinds of incentives or disincentives would this create? What would be the results...”
URL: https://freerepublic.com/focus/f-bloggers/4387931/posts
Time Published: 2026-07-14T17:32:41Z
Full Content:
Skip to comments. Posted on 07/14/2026 10:32:41 AM PDT by grundle I asked Grok: “What do you think would happen if Bernie Sanders’ proposal for the government to own 50% of AI companies were to happen? What kinds of incentives or disincentives would this create? What would be the results of these incentives or disincentives?” This is Grok’s answer: If implemented, Sanders’ proposal — a one-time 50% stock “tax” on major AI companies (those hitting ~$200M in annual AI revenue), creating a ~$7 trillion sovereign wealth fund managed by a 7-person presidentially appointed commission with voting shares and board influence — would represent a major shift toward state-directed capitalism in a high-stakes sector. This isn’t full nationalization but gives the public sector effective veto/power-sharing rights (50% ownership) over core decisions in frontier AI, compute, data centers, robotics, etc. New entrants would face the same once they scale. Here’s a breakdown of likely incentives, disincentives, and results, grounded in economic principles, historical parallels (e.g., state-owned enterprises, sovereign wealth funds like Norway’s or Alaska’s), and innovation dynamics.Short-term effects (implementation shock) Capital flight and legal battles: Founders, VCs, and employees holding equity would see immediate dilution. This is a massive uncompensated transfer — not a traditional tax on profits. Expect lawsuits (takings clause challenges under the 5th Amendment), lobbying, and attempts to restructure (e.g., spinning off non-AI units, relocating HQs, or accelerating foreign listings). Talent and capital could accelerate moves to friendlier jurisdictions (Singapore, UAE, Switzerland, or even China for some applications). Market reaction: AI-heavy stocks (Nvidia, Microsoft, Google, etc.) would tank on announcement due to uncertainty and dilution. Employee stock options lose value, harming retention. Valuation of the fund itself would be contested and volatile. Key incentives and disincentives created Reduced private innovation incentives: Disincentive: Founders and investors risk building something valuable only for the government to claim half upon success. This lowers expected returns dramatically. High-risk, high-reward frontier AI (e.g., AGI pursuits) relies on asymmetric upside for talent and capital. Halving it discourages moonshots. Result: Slower progress on capabilities. Companies shift to safer, incremental applications (e.g., enterprise tools over transformative systems). Less venture funding for startups, as the “exit” or scaling path includes mandatory government partnership. Historical analogy: Heavily regulated or partially state-owned sectors (e.g., European telecom/energy pre-liberalization, or modern critiques of SOEs in many countries) often lag in dynamism. washingtonpost.com Political and bureaucratic incentives dominate: Incentive for commission: The 7-person body gains enormous power to “block harmful decisions” and “push helpful ones.” Definitions of “harm” (job displacement, bias, safety, military use, content moderation, pricing) become politicized. Future administrations could steer AI toward favored constituencies (e.g., union protections, green mandates, censorship, or national security priorities). Disincentive for companies: Executives prioritize regulatory compliance and political favor over pure R&D/market fit. Rent-seeking rises — lobbying the commission becomes as important as technical excellence. Result: Regulatory capture in reverse (government capture of firms), slower decision-making, and potential misalignment. AI safety could improve in some views (more oversight) or worsen (bureaucratic delays on genuine risks). Defense/military AI might advance if aligned with U.S. interests, but commercial/consumer innovation suffers. Parallels in state-influenced tech (e.g., certain European or Chinese models) show more caution but less breakthrough speed. Wealth distribution and fiscal incentives: Incentive: A 5% dividend could yield ~$1,000+ per American annually initially, funding UBI-like payments, healthcare, education, or housing. If AI booms, the fund grows and provides a broad revenue base (like Alaska’s oil fund or Norway’s). Disincentive: Governments face weak incentives for efficiency. Losses (if AI hype deflates or competition erodes margins) fall partly on the public balance sheet. Future dilution requirements (50% of new shares) complicate fundraising/employee comp. Politicians treat the fund as a piggy bank, leading to deficits elsewhere or mismanagement (common in sovereign funds under political pressure). Result: Short-term popular payouts, but potential long-term drag on growth. If AI drives massive productivity, total wealth rises — but the U.S. share of global AI leadership shrinks, reducing future tax base and fund value. Inequality might decrease via dividends, but opportunity and dynamism decline. Talent, competition, and global effects: Top researchers/engineers respond to upside and autonomy. Dilution + bureaucracy pushes talent abroad or to smaller, unregulated players. Open-source or decentralized AI efforts might gain relative advantage. Internationally: U.S. AI edge erodes vs. China (state-directed but without Western rule-of-law constraints) or agile allies. National security risks if control hampers rapid iteration. Longer-term outcomes Optimistic scenario (per proponents): AI advances under democratic guardrails. Public captures more upside, funding social programs and easing disruption (job loss from automation). Sovereign wealth stabilizes economy like resource funds. Pessimistic/base-case scenario (economics of incentives): Innovation slowdown. AI progress fragments and decelerates in the U.S., with breakthroughs happening elsewhere or in gray markets. Bureaucracy adds costs/delays (see historical national champions). Economic growth underperforms counterfactual private-led path. Dividends provide visible benefits but mask opportunity costs (fewer transformative products, lower wages/productivity overall). Precedents like partial state ownership in airlines, energy, or autos often yield inefficiency without eliminating private problems. Unintendeds: Evasion (redefining “AI” businesses), black markets in models, accelerated offshoring, or backlash leading to policy reversal. Employee ownership (as in SpaceX) already shares gains without government control — this overrides that. In summary, this trades private incentives for rapid, decentralized innovation for centralized redistribution and control. AI’s transformative potential amplifies both upsides (shared gains) and downsides (stifled progress, politicization). Evidence from economics favors competitive markets with targeted regulation/taxation over equity seizures for sustaining technological leadership. Outcomes depend heavily on implementation, commission competence, and global competition — but incentives point toward less dynamism. (end of Grok’s answer) TOPICS: Miscellaneous KEYWORDS: nobodyaskedyou; youhavenofriends 1 posted on 07/14/2026 10:32:41 AM PDT by grundle [ Post Reply | Private Reply | View Replies] To: grundle Commies have to commie. Government-owned AI centers would weaponize this data for the benefit of government power and control over citizens. 6 posted on 07/14/2026 11:03:39 AM PDT by MtnClimber (For photos of scenery, wildlife and climbing, click on my screen name for my FR home page.) [ Post Reply | Private Reply | To 1 | View Replies] Disclaimer: Opinions posted on Free Republic are those of the individual posters and do not necessarily represent the opinion of Free Republic or its management. All materials posted herein are protected by copyright law and the exemption for fair use of copyrighted works. Free Republic Browse · Search Bloggers & Personal Topics · Post Article FreeRepublic, LLC, PO BOX 9771, FRESNO, CA 93794 FreeRepublic.com is powered by software copyright 2000-2008 John Robinson This is Grok’s answer: If implemented, Sanders’ proposal — a one-time 50% stock “tax” on major AI companies (those hitting ~$200M in annual AI revenue), creating a ~$7 trillion sovereign wealth fund managed by a 7-person presidentially appointed commission with voting shares and board influence — would represent a major shift toward state-directed capitalism in a high-stakes sector. This isn’t full nationalization but gives the public sector effective veto/power-sharing rights (50% ownership) over core decisions in frontier AI, compute, data centers, robotics, etc. New entrants would face the same once they scale. Here’s a breakdown of likely incentives, disincentives, and results, grounded in economic principles, historical parallels (e.g., state-owned enterprises, sovereign wealth funds like Norway’s or Alaska’s), and innovation dynamics.Short-term effects (implementation shock) Capital flight and legal battles: Founders, VCs, and employees holding equity would see immediate dilution. This is a massive uncompensated transfer — not a traditional tax on profits. Expect lawsuits (takings clause challenges under the 5th Amendment), lobbying, and attempts to restructure (e.g., spinning off non-AI units, relocating HQs, or accelerating foreign listings). Talent and capital could accelerate moves to friendlier jurisdictions (Singapore, UAE, Switzerland, or even China for some applications). Market reaction: AI-heavy stocks (Nvidia, Microsoft, Google, etc.) would tank on announcement due to uncertainty and dilution. Employee stock options lose value, harming retention. Valuation of the fund itself would be contested and volatile. Key incentives and disincentives created Reduced private innovation incentives: Disincentive: Founders and investors risk building something valuable only for the government to claim half upon success. This lowers expected returns dramatically. High-risk, high-reward frontier AI (e.g., AGI pursuits) relies on asymmetric upside for talent and capital. Halving it discourages moonshots. Result: Slower progress on capabilities. Companies shift to safer, incremental applications (e.g., enterprise tools over transformative systems). Less venture funding for startups, as the “exit” or scaling path includes mandatory government partnership. Historical analogy: Heavily regulated or partially state-owned sectors (e.g., European telecom/energy pre-liberalization, or modern critiques of SOEs in many countries) often lag in dynamism. washingtonpost.com Political and bureaucratic incentives dominate: Incentive for commission: The 7-person body gains enormous power to “block harmful decisions” and “push helpful ones.” Definitions of “harm” (job displacement, bias, safety, military use, content moderation, pricing) become politicized. Future administrations could steer AI toward favored constituencies (e.g., union protections, green mandates, censorship, or national security priorities). Disincentive for companies: Executives prioritize regulatory compliance and political favor over pure R&D/market fit. Rent-seeking rises — lobbying the commission becomes as important as technical excellence. Result: Regulatory capture in reverse (government capture of firms), slower decision-making, and potential misalignment. AI safety could improve in some views (more oversight) or worsen (bureaucratic delays on genuine risks). Defense/military AI might advance if aligned with U.S. interests, but commercial/consumer innovation suffers. Parallels in state-influenced tech (e.g., certain European or Chinese models) show more caution but less breakthrough speed. Wealth distribution and fiscal incentives: Incentive: A 5% dividend could yield ~$1,000+ per American annually initially, funding UBI-like payments, healthcare, education, or housing. If AI booms, the fund grows and provides a broad revenue base (like Alaska’s oil fund or Norway’s). Disincentive: Governments face weak incentives for efficiency. Losses (if AI hype deflates or competition erodes margins) fall partly on the public balance sheet. Future dilution requirements (50% of new shares) complicate fundraising/employee comp. Politicians treat the fund as a piggy bank, leading to deficits elsewhere or mismanagement (common in sovereign funds under political pressure). Result: Short-term popular payouts, but potential long-term drag on growth. If AI drives massive productivity, total wealth rises — but the U.S. share of global AI leadership shrinks, reducing future tax base and fund value. Inequality might decrease via dividends, but opportunity and dynamism decline. Talent, competition, and global effects: Top researchers/engineers respond to upside and autonomy. Dilution + bureaucracy pushes talent abroad or to smaller, unregulated players. Open-source or decentralized AI efforts might gain relative advantage. Internationally: U.S. AI edge erodes vs. China (state-directed but without Western rule-of-law constraints) or agile allies. National security risks if control hampers rapid iteration. Longer-term outcomes Optimistic scenario (per proponents): AI advances under democratic guardrails. Public captures more upside, funding social programs and easing disruption (job loss from automation). Sovereign wealth stabilizes economy like resource funds. Pessimistic/base-case scenario (economics of incentives): Innovation slowdown. AI progress fragments and decelerates in the U.S., with breakthroughs happening elsewhere or in gray markets. Bureaucracy adds costs/delays (see historical national champions). Economic growth underperforms counterfactual private-led path. Dividends provide visible benefits but mask opportunity costs (fewer transformative products, lower wages/productivity overall). Precedents like partial state ownership in airlines, energy, or autos often yield inefficiency without eliminating private problems. Unintendeds: Evasion (redefining “AI” businesses), black markets in models, accelerated offshoring, or backlash leading to policy reversal. Employee ownership (as in SpaceX) already shares gains without government control — this overrides that. In summary, this trades private incentives for rapid, decentralized innovation for centralized redistribution and control. AI’s transformative potential amplifies both upsides (shared gains) and downsides (stifled progress, politicization). Evidence from economics favors competitive markets with targeted regulation/taxation over equity seizures for sustaining technological leadership. Outcomes depend heavily on implementation, commission competence, and global competition — but incentives point toward less dynamism. Mutually contradictory terms. 14 posted on 07/14/2026 2:30:18 PM PDT by LegendHasIt [ Post Reply | Private Reply | To 3 | View Replies] To: grundle Bernie, run for pres, buys another mansion. what a farse. 15 posted on 07/15/2026 4:02:34 AM PDT by Joe Boucher (Kimber .45 Be Kind.) [ Post Reply | Private Reply | To 1 | View Replies] Disclaimer: Opinions posted on Free Republic are those of the individual posters and do not necessarily represent the opinion of Free Republic or its management. All materials posted herein are protected by copyright law and the exemption for fair use of copyrighted works. Free Republic Browse · Search Bloggers & Personal Topics · Post Article FreeRepublic, LLC, PO BOX 9771, FRESNO, CA 93794 FreeRepublic.com is powered by software copyright 2000-2008 John Robinson This isn’t full nationalization but gives the public sector effective veto/power-sharing rights (50% ownership) over core decisions in frontier AI, compute, data centers, robotics, etc. New entrants would face the same once they scale. Here’s a breakdown of likely incentives, disincentives, and results, grounded in economic principles, historical parallels (e.g., state-owned enterprises, sovereign wealth funds like Norway’s or Alaska’s), and innovation dynamics.Short-term effects (implementation shock) Capital flight and legal battles: Founders, VCs, and employees holding equity would see immediate dilution. This is a massive uncompensated transfer — not a traditional tax on profits. Expect lawsuits (takings clause challenges under the 5th Amendment), lobbying, and attempts to restructure (e.g., spinning off non-AI units, relocating HQs, or accelerating foreign listings). Talent and capital could accelerate moves to friendlier jurisdictions (Singapore, UAE, Switzerland, or even China for some applications). Market reaction: AI-heavy stocks (Nvidia, Microsoft, Google, etc.) would tank on announcement due to uncertainty and dilution. Employee stock options lose value, harming retention. Valuation of the fund itself would be contested and volatile. Key incentives and disincentives created Reduced private innovation incentives: Disincentive: Founders and investors risk building something valuable only for the government to claim half upon success. This lowers expected returns dramatically. High-risk, high-reward frontier AI (e.g., AGI pursuits) relies on asymmetric upside for talent and capital. Halving it discourages moonshots. Result: Slower progress on capabilities. Companies shift to safer, incremental applications (e.g., enterprise tools over transformative systems). Less venture funding for startups, as the “exit” or scaling path includes mandatory government partnership. Historical analogy: Heavily regulated or partially state-owned sectors (e.g., European telecom/energy pre-liberalization, or modern critiques of SOEs in many countries) often lag in dynamism. washingtonpost.com Political and bureaucratic incentives dominate: Incentive for commission: The 7-person body gains enormous power to “block harmful decisions” and “push helpful ones.” Definitions of “harm” (job displacement, bias, safety, military use, content moderation, pricing) become politicized. Future administrations could steer AI toward favored constituencies (e.g., union protections, green mandates, censorship, or national security priorities). Disincentive for companies: Executives prioritize regulatory compliance and political favor over pure R&D/market fit. Rent-seeking rises — lobbying the commission becomes as important as technical excellence. Result: Regulatory capture in reverse (government capture of firms), slower decision-making, and potential misalignment. AI safety could improve in some views (more oversight) or worsen (bureaucratic delays on genuine risks). Defense/military AI might advance if aligned with U.S. interests, but commercial/consumer innovation suffers. Parallels in state-influenced tech (e.g., certain European or Chinese models) show more caution but less breakthrough speed. Wealth distribution and fiscal incentives: Incentive: A 5% dividend could yield ~$1,000+ per American annually initially, funding UBI-like payments, healthcare, education, or housing. If AI booms, the fund grows and provides a broad revenue base (like Alaska’s oil fund or Norway’s). Disincentive: Governments face weak incentives for efficiency. Losses (if AI hype deflates or competition erodes margins) fall partly on the public balance sheet. Future dilution requirements (50% of new shares) complicate fundraising/employee comp. Politicians treat the fund as a piggy bank, leading to deficits elsewhere or mismanagement (common in sovereign funds under political pressure). Result: Short-term popular payouts, but potential long-term drag on growth. If AI drives massive productivity, total wealth rises — but the U.S. share of global AI leadership shrinks, reducing future tax base and fund value. Inequality might decrease via dividends, but opportunity and dynamism decline. Talent, competition, and global effects: Top researchers/engineers respond to upside and autonomy. Dilution + bureaucracy pushes talent abroad or to smaller, unregulated players. Open-source or decentralized AI efforts might gain relative advantage. Internationally: U.S. AI edge erodes vs. China (state-directed but without Western rule-of-law constraints) or agile allies. National security risks if control hampers rapid iteration. Longer-term outcomes Optimistic scenario (per proponents): AI advances under democratic guardrails. Public captures more upside, funding social programs and easing disruption (job loss from automation). Sovereign wealth stabilizes economy like resource funds. Pessimistic/base-case scenario (economics of incentives): Innovation slowdown. AI progress fragments and decelerates in the U.S., with breakthroughs happening elsewhere or in gray markets. Bureaucracy adds costs/delays (see historical national champions). Economic growth underperforms counterfactual private-led path. Dividends provide visible benefits but mask opportunity costs (fewer transformative products, lower wages/productivity overall). Precedents like partial state ownership in airlines, energy, or autos often yield inefficiency without eliminating private problems. Unintendeds: Evasion (redefining “AI” businesses), black markets in models, accelerated offshoring, or backlash leading to policy reversal. Employee ownership (as in SpaceX) already shares gains without government control — this overrides that. In summary, this trades private incentives for rapid, decentralized innovation for centralized redistribution and control. AI’s transformative potential amplifies both upsides (shared gains) and downsides (stifled progress, politicization). Evidence from economics favors competitive markets with targeted regulation/taxation over equity seizures for sustaining technological leadership. Outcomes depend heavily on implementation, commission competence, and global competition — but incentives point toward less dynamism. (end of Grok’s answer) TOPICS: Miscellaneous KEYWORDS: nobodyaskedyou; youhavenofriends 1 posted on 07/14/2026 10:32:41 AM PDT by grundle [ Post Reply | Private Reply | View Replies] To: grundle Commies have to commie. Mutually contradictory terms. 14 posted on 07/14/2026 2:30:18 PM PDT by LegendHasIt [ Post Reply | Private Reply | To 3 | View Replies] To: grundle Bernie, run for pres, buys another mansion. what a farse. 15 posted on 07/15/2026 4:02:34 AM PDT by Joe Boucher (Kimber .45 Be Kind.) [ Post Reply | Private Reply | To 1 | View Replies] Disclaimer: Opinions posted on Free Republic are those of the individual posters and do not necessarily represent the opinion of Free Republic or its management. All materials posted herein are protected by copyright law and the exemption for fair use of copyrighted works. Free Republic Browse · Search Bloggers & Personal Topics · Post Article FreeRepublic, LLC, PO BOX 9771, FRESNO, CA 93794 FreeRepublic.com is powered by software copyright 2000-2008 John Robinson Reduced private innovation incentives: Disincentive: Founders and investors risk building something valuable only for the government to claim half upon success. This lowers expected returns dramatically. High-risk, high-reward frontier AI (e.g., AGI pursuits) relies on asymmetric upside for talent and capital. Halving it discourages moonshots. Result: Slower progress on capabilities. Companies shift to safer, incremental applications (e.g., enterprise tools over transformative systems). Less venture funding for startups, as the “exit” or scaling path includes mandatory government partnership. Historical analogy: Heavily regulated or partially state-owned sectors (e.g., European telecom/energy pre-liberalization, or modern critiques of SOEs in many countries) often lag in dynamism. washingtonpost.com Political and bureaucratic incentives dominate: Incentive for commission: The 7-person body gains enormous power to “block harmful decisions” and “push helpful ones.” Definitions of “harm” (job displacement, bias, safety, military use, content moderation, pricing) become politicized. Future administrations could steer AI toward favored constituencies (e.g., union protections, green mandates, censorship, or national security priorities). Disincentive for companies: Executives prioritize regulatory compliance and political favor over pure R&D/market fit. Rent-seeking rises — lobbying the commission becomes as important as technical excellence. Result: Regulatory capture in reverse (government capture of firms), slower decision-making, and potential misalignment. AI safety could improve in some views (more oversight) or worsen (bureaucratic delays on genuine risks). Defense/military AI might advance if aligned with U.S. interests, but commercial/consumer innovation suffers. Parallels in state-influenced tech (e.g., certain European or Chinese models) show more caution but less breakthrough speed. Wealth distribution and fiscal incentives: Incentive: A 5% dividend could yield ~$1,000+ per American annually initially, funding UBI-like payments, healthcare, education, or housing. If AI booms, the fund grows and provides a broad revenue base (like Alaska’s oil fund or Norway’s). Disincentive: Governments face weak incentives for efficiency. Losses (if AI hype deflates or competition erodes margins) fall partly on the public balance sheet. Future dilution requirements (50% of new shares) complicate fundraising/employee comp. Politicians treat the fund as a piggy bank, leading to deficits elsewhere or mismanagement (common in sovereign funds under political pressure). Result: Short-term popular payouts, but potential long-term drag on growth. If AI drives massive productivity, total wealth rises — but the U.S. share of global AI leadership shrinks, reducing future tax base and fund value. Inequality might decrease via dividends, but opportunity and dynamism decline. Talent, competition, and global effects: Top researchers/engineers respond to upside and autonomy. Dilution + bureaucracy pushes talent abroad or to smaller, unregulated players. Open-source or decentralized AI efforts might gain relative advantage. Internationally: U.S. AI edge erodes vs. China (state-directed but without Western rule-of-law constraints) or agile allies. National security risks if control hampers rapid iteration. Longer-term outcomes Optimistic scenario (per proponents): AI advances under democratic guardrails. Public captures more upside, funding social programs and easing disruption (job loss from automation). Sovereign wealth stabilizes economy like resource funds. Pessimistic/base-case scenario (economics of incentives): Innovation slowdown. AI progress fragments and decelerates in the U.S., with breakthroughs happening elsewhere or in gray markets. Bureaucracy adds costs/delays (see historical national champions). Economic growth underperforms counterfactual private-led path. Dividends provide visible benefits but mask opportunity costs (fewer transformative products, lower wages/productivity overall). Precedents like partial state ownership in airlines, energy, or autos often yield inefficiency without eliminating private problems. Unintendeds: Evasion (redefining “AI” businesses), black markets in models, accelerated offshoring, or backlash leading to policy reversal. Employee ownership (as in SpaceX) already shares gains without government control — this overrides that. In summary, this trades private incentives for rapid, decentralized innovation for centralized redistribution and control. AI’s transformative potential amplifies both upsides (shared gains) and downsides (stifled progress, politicization). Evidence from economics favors competitive markets with targeted regulation/taxation over equity seizures for sustaining technological leadership. Outcomes depend heavily on implementation, commission competence, and global competition — but incentives point toward less dynamism. (end of Grok’s answer) TOPICS: Miscellaneous KEYWORDS: nobodyaskedyou; youhavenofriends 1 posted on 07/14/2026 10:32:41 AM PDT by grundle [ Post Reply | Private Reply | View Replies] To: grundle Commies have to commie. [ Post Reply | Private Reply | To 1 | View Replies] Disclaimer: Opinions posted on Free Republic are those of the individual posters and do not necessarily represent the opinion of Free Republic or its management. All materials posted herein are protected by copyright law and the exemption for fair use of copyrighted works. Free Republic Browse · Search Bloggers & Personal Topics · Post Article FreeRepublic, LLC, PO BOX 9771, FRESNO, CA 93794 FreeRepublic.com is powered by software copyright 2000-2008 John Robinson Longer-term outcomes Optimistic scenario (per proponents): AI advances under democratic guardrails. Public captures more upside, funding social programs and easing disruption (job loss from automation). Sovereign wealth stabilizes economy like resource funds. Pessimistic/base-case scenario (economics of incentives): Innovation slowdown. AI progress fragments and decelerates in the U.S., with breakthroughs happening elsewhere or in gray markets. Bureaucracy adds costs/delays (see historical national champions). Economic growth underperforms counterfactual private-led path. Dividends provide visible benefits but mask opportunity costs (fewer transformative products, lower wages/productivity overall). Precedents like partial state ownership in airlines, energy, or autos often yield inefficiency without eliminating private problems. Unintendeds: Evasion (redefining “AI” businesses), black markets in models, accelerated offshoring, or backlash leading to policy reversal. Employee ownership (as in SpaceX) already shares gains without government control — this overrides that. In summary, this trades private incentives for rapid, decentralized innovation for centralized redistribution and control. AI’s transformative potential amplifies both upsides (shared gains) and downsides (stifled progress, politicization). Evidence from economics favors competitive markets with targeted regulation/taxation over equity seizures for sustaining technological leadership. Outcomes depend heavily on implementation, commission competence, and global competition — but incentives point toward less dynamism. Title: Uber’s $150 Billion Platform Is Entering a New Phase of Profitability
URL: https://247wallst.com/investing/2026/07/14/ubers-150-billion-platform-is-entering-a-new-phase-of-profitability/
Time Published: 2026-07-14T17:06:03Z
Full Content:
Investing UBER's operating income surged 57% year over year to $1.9 billion, with non-GAAP EPS growing at more than twice the pace of bookings. Uber One's 50 million members now drive over half of all bookings, with subscribers spending 3x more than non-members. Analysts carry a $104 price target while CFO Balaji Krishnamurthy and multiple executives bought shares in June at $73. Act now: the analyst who called NVIDIA in 2010 just named his top 10 AI stocks — and Uber didn't make the cut. Grab the names FREE today. Sending You to Google News in 3 © Spencer Platt / Getty Images News via Getty Images Uber (NYSE:UBER | UBER Price Prediction) now carries a market capitalization of roughly $151.5 billion, backed by a platform that pushed $53.72 billion in Gross Bookings through its apps in a single quarter. Uber has scaled into a $150 billion consumer platform reporting real operating income, and the most recent quarter shows why the market is starting to price it that way. The scale behind the market cap is what makes the profitability turn credible. In Q1 fiscal 2026, reported May 6, 2026, Uber ran 3.6 billion trips across 199 million Monthly Active Platform Consumers, with trips up 20% year over year and audience up 17%. Gross Bookings climbed 25%. Revenue reached $13.203 billion, just missing the $13.263 billion estimate by 0.45%, a gap the company attributes to a roughly 9 percentage-point headwind from business model changes. The margin story is what pushes this into a new phase. The company’s operating income hit $1.923 billion, up 56.6% year over year. Additionally, adjusted EBITDA margin on Gross Bookings widened to 4.6% from 4.4%, and non-GAAP operating income margin expanded to 3.5% from 3.1%. Non-GAAP EPS came in at $0.72, beating the $0.7133 estimate and growing 44% year over year, more than double the pace of bookings growth. While GAAP net income of $263 million fell 85.19%, that swing came from a $1.50 billion pre-tax mark on equity investments, with the operating business unaffected. Shares closed at $74.43 on July 2, 2026, up 2.44% on the day and 3.92% over the past month. The year-to-date picture is weaker, with the stock down 8.91% from $81.71 at year-end 2025, and off 19.14% over the trailing year from $92.05. Post-earnings, the stock traded at $77.14 one hour after the filing before settling to $70.71 thirty days later. I think the bull case around Uber rests on operating leverage that is showing up in every line the market cares about. CEO Dara Khosrowshahi told investors on the call: “Importantly, we’re scaling this growth profitably. Non-GAAP EPS increased 44% year-over-year, more than twice as fast as our bookings growth, driven by disciplined cost management and operating leverage.” That leverage is being reinforced by a subscription flywheel. Uber One now has 50 million members, up from 30 million at the end of the prior year, growing 50% year on year and driving over 50% of bookings. Members spend 3x more than non-members. Capital return is now part of the equation. Uber repurchased $3.011 billion of stock in Q1 alone, on top of $6.523 billion in buybacks during full-year 2025. Free cash flow reached $2.286 billion in the quarter, and $9.763 billion for full-year 2025, up 41.6%. The balance sheet backs it up, with debt-to-equity at 0.45, net debt to EBITDA at 0.79, and interest coverage of 12.65. Return on equity sits at 41.37%. Insiders have been buying into the pullback. CFO Balaji Krishnamurthy and executives Tony West, Jill Hazelbaker, Andrew MacDonald, and Glen Ceremony all acquired shares in June 2026 at a reference price of $73.25, below the April level of $76.48. Analyst sentiment lines up behind Uber, with analyst posting 10 strong buys, 36 buys, 5 holds, and 1 sell, and a target price of $104.53. For long-term holders, the $150 billion valuation now sits on top of a business generating margin expansion, buybacks, and a subscription base that is compounding. Uber’s Q2 guidance calls for Gross Bookings of $56.25 billion to $57.75 billion, non-GAAP EPS of $0.78 to $0.82 (growth of 31% to 38%), and adjusted EBITDA of $2.70 billion to $2.80 billion. Thus, I think the company’s Q2 2026 report is the next reveal. If EPS growth continues to run at more than twice the pace of bookings, the $150 billion platform stops being a ceiling and starts looking like a floor. Contact [email protected] for any questions or corrections. Title: AVGO Dragged You Through a 30% Plunge for 16%. QQQ Just Went Up 18%
URL: https://247wallst.com/investing/etf/2026/07/14/avgo-dragged-you-through-a-30-plunge-for-16-qqq-just-went-up-18/
Time Published: 2026-07-14T16:45:38Z
Full Content:
ETF QQQ beat AVGO year-to-date in 2026, turning $10,000 into $11,810 versus $11,599, capturing the same AI gains without single-stock risk. Rackspace plunged 34% in a single session, proving concentration cuts both ways while QQQ spreads risk across 100 stocks for 0.20%. Are you ahead, or behind on retirement? SmartAsset's free tool can match you with a financial advisor in minutes to help you answer that today. Each advisor has been carefully vetted, and must act in your best interests. Don't waste another minute; learn more here. Sending You to Google News in 3 © Wikimedia Commons Your feed is full of Broadcom. The stock chart is a staircase, the AI headlines keep coming, and Hock Tan keeps guiding higher. You didn’t buy any. You feel behind. You’re not. From December 31, 2025 through July 10, 2026, Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO | AVGO Price Prediction) returned 15.99% on a year-to-date basis. Over that same window, the Invesco QQQ Trust (NASDAQ:QQQ), the ETF tracking the NASDAQ-100 index, returned 18.10%. The boring basket beat the hot ticker. Anchor it to real money. A $10,000 stake in AVGO on the last trading day of 2025 was worth roughly $11,599 as of July 10, 2026. The same $10,000 in QQQ was worth about $11,810. Different route, better destination, one-stock risk skipped. Zoom out and Broadcom is still the sexier chart. Over the trailing twelve months AVGO is up 46.3% against QQQ at 30.62%. But the story most retail investors are telling themselves right now is a 2026 story, and in the 2026 story the index quietly won. The reason both charts point up is the same reason: the AI infrastructure buildout. Broadcom’s Q2 fiscal 2026 revenue jumped 47.9% year over year to $22.187 billion, with AI semiconductor revenue alone hitting $10.80 billion, up 143% year over year. Management guided Q3 AI revenue to $16.0 billion, more than 200% YoY growth. CEO Hock Tan called it “record revenue, operating profit and free cash flow” driven by custom AI accelerators and networking silicon for hyperscalers. That check being written by the hyperscalers doesn’t land only at one address. It lands across the entire NASDAQ-100 mega-cap tech complex: the chip designers, the foundry customers, the cloud platforms buying the gear, the software layer running on top of it. QQQ owns that theme as a basket of roughly 100 of the largest non-financial companies listed on Nasdaq. Owning the basket captured whichever name printed the loudest AI number. Yes, Broadcom holders had the higher-conviction ride. They also had to sit through it. AVGO’s beta is 1.462 and its 52-week range runs from $267.60 to $494.18. Reddit sentiment on the name whipped from bullish 65 around the Apple deal in early June to very-bearish 18 by June 23. That is the price of admission for a single stock, even a good one. And single-stock risk isn’t hypothetical. On the July 9, 2026 tape, one NYSE-listed hot name, Rackspace (NYSE:RXT) dropped 33.59% in a single session on 59 million shares of volume. Different company, different story, same lesson: concentration cuts both ways. QQQ spreads that risk across its full basket for an expense ratio of roughly 0.20%. You gave up the top of the trade to skip the bottom of it. This is the quiet argument for indexing the theme instead of chasing the ticker. You don’t need to have called Broadcom’s AI accelerator ramp, timed the Apple partnership, or held through the mid-June sentiment swoon to have participated. You needed exposure to the force behind all of it, and you needed to leave it alone. Broadcom may keep running. It may not. If you’re kicking yourself for missing AVGO in 2026, look at your QQQ line again. The tide did the work. Owning a process that captures tides beats guessing which fish will jump highest, and it costs a lot less sleep. Contact [email protected] for any questions or corrections. He returned to Amazon, his former employer, after the experimental project was cancelled. This experience significantly altered his views on career growth and job security at the big tech companies.
